title: Nasya: Why Ayurveda Puts Oil in Your Nose
excerpt: Nasya, the ancient practice of putting oil into your nostrils, is one of Ayurveda's most overlooked daily rituals. A few drops in the morning can clear your head, ease sinus congestion, and help you sleep more soundly.
body_html:
that morning fog in your head

You wake up with a heavy feeling behind your eyes. Your nose is a little blocked even though you are not sick. Your thoughts feel slow, and by afternoon a dull ache sits above your eyebrows. Many people carry this low-grade congestion for years, assuming it is just dust or the weather. Ayurveda has a precise name for what is happening and a precise remedy for it.

The remedy is Nasya, pronounced nuss-ya, which simply means nasal administration. It is the practice of putting a small amount of medicated oil, ghee, or herbal preparation into each nostril so that it travels upward toward the head. Classical texts describe the nose as the doorway to the brain and to all the structures of the head, a concept that aligns surprisingly well with what modern anatomy tells us about the nasal passage and its proximity to the sinuses, the olfactory nerves, and the cerebrospinal fluid pathway.

the nose as the shira dvara, the gateway to the head

what the classical texts say

The Ashtanga Hridaya, one of the three foundational Ayurvedic texts, devotes considerable attention to Nasya as part of the daily routine called dinacharya. It places Nasya among the essential morning practices alongside oil pulling and ear drops, recommending it as a way to keep the head, eyes, ears, nose, and throat clear and strong (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). The same text describes the nose as the shira dvara, the gateway to the head, which is why substances applied there are understood to act on structures far above the nostrils.

The Caraka Samhita discusses Nasya in the context of both preventive care and therapeutic treatment, describing various preparations suited to different conditions (Caraka Samhita, Siddhisthana). It distinguishes between a light daily Nasya for general health and more intensive therapeutic Nasya supervised by a vaidya for deeper imbalances. Sushruta Samhita similarly outlines the types of Nasya and the conditions for which each is appropriate, including disorders of the head, vision, and voice (Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsasthana).

The oil most commonly recommended for home use is Anu Taila, a classical preparation described in the Ashtanga Hridaya and other texts. It contains sesame oil as a base along with a careful blend of herbs. For everyday preventive Nasya, plain sesame oil or plain cow's ghee also has a long tradition of use and is gentle enough for most people.

how to do nasya at home

The classical texts recommend doing Nasya in the morning after brushing your teeth but before eating, ideally after a gentle face wash with warm water. Lie down on a flat surface and tilt your head back so that your nostrils face upward. Place two to four drops of warm Anu Taila, sesame oil, or ghee into each nostril. Breathe gently through the nose, allowing the oil to travel inward. Remain lying down for a minute or two, then sit up slowly. You may notice the oil reaching the back of your throat; spit it out rather than swallowing.

The Ashtanga Hridaya advises warming the oil before use, which you can do by placing the bottle in a cup of hot water for a few minutes. Cold oil is considered less effective and harder for the tissues to absorb. Most texts suggest doing daily preventive Nasya with just two drops per nostril, which is quite conservative and very manageable for a beginner.

Timing matters in the traditional framework. Nasya is avoided immediately after eating, after a bath, during fever, during menstruation, in very young children, and during pregnancy. These cautions are consistent across the Caraka Samhita and Ashtanga Hridaya. On a typical healthy morning, it takes less than five minutes and the benefits tend to build over weeks rather than being dramatic on the first day.

when to see a doctor or vaidya

Nasya is a gentle practice when done correctly at home with plain sesame oil or ghee in the amounts described above. However, it is not suitable for everyone in every situation. See a qualified Ayurvedic vaidya before starting Nasya if you have chronic sinusitis, nasal polyps, a deviated septum, frequent nosebleeds, or a history of head injury. Seek medical attention promptly if you experience nasal bleeding that does not stop, sudden changes in vision or hearing, severe headache, fever with facial pain, or any symptom that concerns you. Therapeutic Nasya using stronger herbal preparations should only be done under the direct supervision of a trained practitioner. The information in this article is for general educational purposes and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for advice from a qualified doctor or vaidya who knows your individual health situation.

Try This Today

  • Two-drop morning Nasya: After brushing your teeth, warm a small bottle of sesame oil in a cup of hot water for two minutes. Lie back, tilt your head, and place two drops in each nostril. Breathe gently through the nose and rest for one minute before sitting up.
  • Warm the oil first: Cold oil is less effective and can feel jarring. Always check the temperature on your inner wrist before use. It should feel comfortably warm, not hot.
  • Follow with gentle nasal breathing: After the drops, take five slow breaths through the nose before getting up. This helps the oil travel toward the sinuses and the upper passages.
  • Keep to two to four drops: For daily preventive use at home, two drops per nostril is enough. More is not better for routine practice and the classical texts are consistent on this point.

metafields.dek: Nasya, the ancient practice of putting oil into your nostrils, is one of Ayurveda's most overlooked daily rituals. A few drops in the morning can clear your head, ease sinus congestion, and help you sleep more soundly.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Nasya means placing a few drops of oil in each nostril every morning to keep the head, sinuses, and mind clear.
Anu Taila is the classical preparation most recommended; plain warm sesame oil or ghee works well for everyday home use.
Skip Nasya during fever, pregnancy, menstruation, or immediately after a meal; see a doctor for any acute symptoms, and consult a vaidya before therapeutic use.

glossary_terms.definition: Nasal administration of oil, ghee, or herbal preparations; one of the five Panchakarma therapies and also a daily dinacharya practice
A classical medicated sesame-oil preparation specifically formulated for Nasya, described in the Ashtanga Hridaya
Daily routine; the Ayurvedic framework of morning and evening practices that maintain health over time
Gateway to the head; the classical description of the nose as the entry point through which medicines reach the head
The five classical cleansing therapies in Ayurveda, of which Nasya is one

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Nasya" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Nasya is defined as: Nasal administration of oil, ghee, or herbal preparations; one of the five Panchakarma therapies and also a daily dinacharya practice

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to two-drop morning nasya. After brushing your teeth, warm a small bottle of sesame oil in a cup of hot water for two minutes. Lie back, tilt your head, and place two drops in each nostril. Breathe gently through the nose and rest for one minute before sitting up.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to warm the oil first. Cold oil is less effective and can feel jarring. Always check the temperature on your inner wrist before use. It should feel comfortably warm, not hot.
